data.title: Stock index futures slump as concerns on health of regional banks rattle sentiments
data.description:
Stock index futures were deeply in the red on Friday, as traders grew concerned about the health of regional banks on credit risk worries.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) -1.3%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) -1.5% and Dow futures (INDU) -1%.

Wall Street's major averages closed lower on Thursday amid increased volatility due to the ongoing U.S.-China trade conflict and bad loan reports in banks, while investors continued to assess earnings reports and the ongoing U.S. government shutdown.

Stocks of investment bank Jefferies Financial (JEF) and regional banks Zions Bancorporation (NASDAQ:ZION), and Western Alliance (WAL) remained down on Friday as investors worried about the quality of loans and investments made by the firms.

"Market sentiment saw a sharp deterioration over the past 24 hours as news of bad loans at two U.S. regional lenders triggered broader concerns about credit quality, leading U.S. bank stocks to their worst day since early April while the S&P 500 sank -0.63%," Deutsche Bank's Peter Sidorov said. 

"An initially positive risk mood turned during the U.S. session yesterday after news that Zions Bancorp made a $50M charge-off, while Western Alliance alleged it also suffered from fraud on loans to the same borrowers linked to distressed commercial mortgages. While this was an ostensibly isolated story at two banks, each with less than $10bn market cap, the event drew inevitable comparisons to the regional bank stress that followed the collapse of SVB in March 2023," Sidorov added.

The KBW Nasdaq Regional Banking Index (KRX) closed down 6.3% on Thursday trading, its biggest drop since April, following the disclosures by two banks of irregularities related to some loans.

"Financial markets seem to have been exhibiting some concerns about credit risk. However, credit has not been a limiting factor for growth, and middle-income households still have strong balance sheets with low debt levels," UBS' Paul Donovan said. 

The language of Federal Reserve speakers is still focused on cutting rates. Credit concerns should not necessarily be dismissed but need to be set in a proper context, Donovan added.

Meanwhile, the U.S. government shutdown continued to grind on as it entered its third week.

The economic calendar is quiet on the day, with mainly St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem slated to speak.

data.title: Novo Nordisk, Eli Lilly slide after Trump pledges 'much lower' Ozempic price
data.description:
Danish drugmaker Novo Nordisk (NVO) slipped 6% in early trading on Friday after U.S. President Donald Trump said on Thursday that the price of the company's top-selling weight-loss medication would be reduced.

Trump made the comments during a White House event on fertility treatments and drug pricing, as per reports.

He said the price of the blockbuster diabetes drug Ozempic could come down to just $150 a month, as per Bloomberg News.

The U.S. list price of Ozempic, which Trump called “the fat loss drug,” is roughly $1,000 for a month’s supply. Trump told reporters on Thursday that the cost of the drug, made by Novo, will soon be “much lower.”

Eli Lilly (LLY), which sells similar treatments, including Mounjaro and Zepbound, also ticked 4% lower.

data.title: Coca-Cola considers $1 billion IPO of Indian bottling unit - report
data.description:
• Coca-Cola (NYSE:KO) is said to be considering taking its Indian bottling unit public in a deal that may fetch $1 billion.
  • The company has met with bankers to discuss listing Hindustan Coca-Cola Beverages, Bloomberg News reported on Friday, adding that it would value the unit at $10 billion.
  • The discussions are ongoing, and key details such as the timing, structure, and size of the offering may still evolve, the report added. Coca-Cola (NYSE:KO) has so far not commented on the development.

data.title: CME eyes new sports event contracts to rival Kalshi, Polymarket - report
data.description:
CME (NASDAQ:CME) is planning to launch financial contracts tied to sports games and economic indicators, rivaling popular prediction markets Kalshi and Polymarket, people familiar with the matter told Bloomberg.

The contracts may be rolled out by year-end through futures commission merchants, including the one CME (NASDAQ:CME) is setting up with FanDuel (DUEL). FanDuel is owned by Flutter Entertainment (FLUT).

CME (CME) could also offer the contracts on other similar platforms, opening it up to traditional retail brokerages. The plans could still change, according to the report.

Prediction markets, which allow users to bet on outcomes in a variety of real-world events, have been rapidly gaining popularity since the 2024 U.S. presidential election. 

Polymarket, the largest prediction market, last week secured a $2B investment from NYSE-owner Intercontinental Exchange (ICE). The deal valued Polymarket at about $8B pre-investment, compared to its $1B valuation just two months ago.

Days after Polymarket's announcement, Kalshi said it raised over $300M at a $5B valuation. Kalshi had been valued at $2B back in June.

data.title: Chinese carmaker BYD to recall record number of cars over design, battery issues
data.description:
BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDF) announced its largest vehicle recall to date, affecting more than 115,000 cars due to safety-related design and battery defects identified by China’s State Administration for Market Regulation.

BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) filed a plan to recall 44,535 units of the Tang series manufactured between March 2015 and July 2017 due to certain component design flaws may cause abnormal function.

Meanwhile, 71,248 units of the Yuan Pro electric vehicles, produced from February 2021 to August 2022, are being recalled over due to manufacturing issues affecting battery installation.

Previously, in January, the company made a recall of 6,843 Fangchengbao Bao 5 plug-in hybrid off-road SUVs citing fire risks.

data.title: Tomra Systems ASA Non-GAAP EPS of EUR 0.05, revenue of EUR 306M
data.description:
• Tomra Systems ASA press release (OTCPK:TMRAY): Q3 Non-GAAP EPS of EUR0.05.
  • Revenue of EUR306M (-6.1% Y/Y).
  • Cash flow from operations amounted to EUR64 M compared to EUR99 M.
  • Recycling order intake of EUR42M and order backlog of EUR109 M, down 30% and 19% respectively.
  • Food order intake of EUR77 M and order backlog of EUR138 M, up 6% and 21% respectively.

data.title: U.S. Chamber of Commerce sues Trump admin over $100,000 H-1B visa fees
data.description:
The U.S. Chamber of Commerce has filed a major lawsuit against the Trump administration over a $100,000 annual fee on H-1B visas, calling it unlawful and economically damaging to American businesses.

The U.S. Chamber of Commerce—the nation’s largest business lobby, representing companies like Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), and Alphabet (GOOG)—filed the lawsuit in federal court in Washington, arguing that the fee is unlawful and “overrides provisions of the Immigration and Nationality Act that govern the H-1B program, including the requirement that fees be based on the costs incurred by the government in processing visas.”

“The new $100,000 visa fee will make it cost-prohibitive for U.S. employers, especially start-ups and small and midsize businesses, to utilize the H-1B program, which was created by Congress expressly to ensure that American businesses of all sizes can access the global talent they need to grow their operations here in the U.S." said Neil Bradley, Executive Vice President and Chief Policy Officer at the U.S. Chamber, in a statement.

Trump introduced the six-figure H-1B visa fee last month through an executive order, drastically raising the cost from the previous range of $2,000–$5,000 to $100,000 per application.

H-1B visas normally have a three-year validity period, with the possibility of a six-year extension and the annual ceiling for the program is 65,000 visas, with an extra 20,000 reserved for those with advanced degrees. 

The admin framed it as an effort to “protect American workers” and curb what it termed visa “abuse,” asserting that the H-1B system had been exploited by companies seeking cheaper foreign labor.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Micron to exit server chips business in China - report
data.description:
• Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) plans to stop supplying server chips to data centers in China after the business failed to recover from a 2023 government ban on its products in critical Chinese infrastructure, Reuters reported.
  • Micron was the first U.S. chipmaker to be targeted by Beijing - a move that was seen as retaliatory for a series of curbs by Washington aimed at impeding tech progress by China's semiconductor industry.
  • Micron will, however, continue to sell to two Chinese customers who have significant data center operations outside China, one of which is laptop maker Lenovo (OTCPK:LNVGY), the report said.
  • The U.S. company, which made $3.4 billion, or 12% of its total revenue, from mainland China in its last business year, will also continue to sell chips to auto and mobile phone sector customers in the world's second-largest economy, one person said.

data.title: FSI announces $10.53M revenue increase in Q3 2025
data.description:
• Flexible Solution International (NYSE:FSI) announced on Thursday an increase in revenue to $10.539M in Q3 2025 from $9.315M in Q3 2024, up approximately 13% YoY growth.
  • The complete financial results will be available after market close on Friday, November 14, 2025.

data.title: Yara International Non-GAAP EPS of $1.33, revenue of $4.11B misses by $180M
data.description:
• Yara International press release (OTCPK:YARIY): Q3 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.33.
  • Revenue of $4.11B (+12.6% Y/Y) misses by $180M.
  • EBITDA excl. special items of 804 MUSD, up 38% from 3Q24.
  • Yara's third-quarter operating cash flow was USD 32 million higher than a year earlier.
  • As of 3Q 2025, the program is ahead of schedule and Yara is now targeting a 4Q 2025 run rate of USD 2,350 million excluding currency effects, which would represent USD 180 million in annual fixed cost savings net of inflation. Capex guidance for 2025 is USD 1.1 billion, USD 250 million lower than original guidance, reflecting continued capital discipline as Yara strictly prioritizes higher paying investment, further supported by lower capex requirement due to portfolio adjustments.
